title: Influenza antivirals
summary: Prescription antivirals such as oseltamivir are relevant to influenza, not ordinary colds. Benefit is greatest when treatment starts early, especially for people at high risk, severely unwell or hospitalised.
evidenceSnapshot: Antivirals can modestly shorten uncomplicated influenza and may reduce complications in higher-risk or severe disease. They work best within 48 hours, but later treatment may still be appropriate for severe/high-risk cases.

interventions.name: Early clinical contact for high-risk influenza
interventions.finding: Allows timely testing and antiviral prescribing.
interventions.practical: Contact a GP, urgent care service or healthdirect promptly when flu symptoms begin in a high-risk person.
interventions.mechanism: Earlier antiviral exposure limits viral replication.
interventions.cautions: Do not wait for severe symptoms if high risk.

interventions.name: Oseltamivir (Tamiflu)
interventions.finding: Prescription neuraminidase inhibitor used for treatment or prevention in selected cases.
interventions.practical: Dose and duration depend on age, weight, kidney function and indication; use exactly as prescribed.
interventions.mechanism: Blocks release of new influenza virus particles.
interventions.cautions: Nausea/vomiting are common; rare neuropsychiatric events have been reported.

interventions.name: Antivirals after 48 hours
interventions.finding: May still help people who are hospitalised, severely ill or at high risk.
interventions.practical: Clinical decision rather than automatic exclusion.
interventions.mechanism: Ongoing viral replication may remain clinically important.
interventions.cautions: Do not self-source or share prescriptions.

practicalSteps: Know whether the person is in a high-risk group before winter illness occurs.
Call early if symptoms are abrupt with fever, aches and marked fatigue.
Continue supportive care; antivirals do not replace hydration, monitoring or emergency assessment.
avoidOrCare: Antivirals do not treat rhinovirus and are not antibiotics.
Pregnant people are a high-risk group and should seek prompt advice for suspected influenza.

myths.claim: Tamiflu cures any cold or flu-like illness.
There is no point after 48 hours.
myths.reality: Early is best, but severe, hospitalised or high-risk patients may still benefit later.

redFlags: Breathing difficulty, chest pain, confusion, blue lips or collapse
Dehydration or inability to take medicines/fluids
Symptoms improve then return with fever or breathlessness
unsettled: The absolute benefit varies by influenza strain, baseline risk, timing and outcome measured.
